content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. Then, integrated filter realizes the state estimation based on the Fig. 1. Architecture of the GNSS/INS-int

content.abstract_or_snippet: me der kürzesten möglichen Wege aller Fahrgäste zu ihren Zielen (direkte Fahrt) dividiert durch die Zahl der insgesamt gefahrenen Ki- lometer, inklusive Betriebs- und Leerfahrten (siehe Liebchen et al., 2021). Andere Berechnungsmethoden ergeben meist Anreize für Umwege, die die Poolingquote er- höhen, dabei aber die gefahrenen Umwege und teilweise auch Gesamtkilometer er- höhen. Insbesondere in Zeit-Räumen schwacher Nachfrage ist eine Bündelung häufig mit großen Umwegen und daher, im Vergleich zum privaten PKW, unattraktiven Reise- zeiten verbunden (Agora Verkehrswende, 2023, S. 40). Aufgrund der oftmals unkla- ren Definition der angegebenen Bündelungsquoten wird hier auf eine Listung ver- schiedener Werte verzichtet und auf die Analyse der vorliegenden Daten in Kapitel 4.2.2 verwiesen. Leerfahrten An- und Abwege zu Nutzer*innen, kurzfristige Buchungsstornierungen oder ähnli- ches führen zu Wegen, die bei Fahrten mit privaten PKW nicht anfallen. Sie erhöhen somit das Verkehrsaufkommen, steigern die Kosten und verschlechtern die Ökobi- lanz der Dienstleistungen. content.title: Yang - A Review of Environmental Perception Technology Based on Multi-Sensor Information Fusion in Autonomous Driving
content.abstract_or_snippet: achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The IMU can provide information about the vehicle’s attitude, velocity, and direction. The IMU helps determine vehicle motion between GNSS updates and ensures continuous tracking of the vehicle’s path, especially in areas where GNSS signals may be temporarily lost, such as tunnels and dense urban areas. The IMU provides real-time feedback on the ve- hicle’s motion, which is critical for continued tracking, especially in dynamic environments. As the positioning error accumulates during the vehicle’s driving, the IMU signal error accumulation will drift over time, so the IMU needs to continuously correct the estimated position. The data fusion of GNSS and IMU can achieve vehicle state estimation and ensure a continuous positioning process. GNSS and IMU data are often fused with other sensors such as LiDAR and radar to improve positioning accuracy and build a complete picture of the vehicle’s surroundings. By fusing these data sources, autonomous vehicles are able to navigate in a variety of environments, even without direct GNSS signals. There are currently several models for combining GNSS and IMU data. (1) GNSS/IMU integration: combining GNSS data with IMU information can create a dead reckoning system that continuously estimates the vehicle’s position when GNSS signals are unavailable. In this model, the IMU uses accelerometers and gyroscopes to track vehicle motion and orientation to fill in the gaps in GNSS data. (2) Tightly coupled system: in this, GNSS and IMU data are fused and processed in real time. This can improve the accuracy of vehicle positioning, es- pecially in challenging environments where GNSS signals are weak or unstable. (3) Loosely coupled system: in this system, GNSS and IMU operate independently and merge the data in the post-processing stage. Compared with the tightly coupled system, this method is less computationally intensive, but less accurate. World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 8 of 34 To mitigate the limitations of GNSS and IMU, these sensors are often fused with other perception technologies, such as LiDAR, radar, and cameras. GNSS/IMU systems are often integrated with LiDAR and radar sensors to enhance the vehicle’s perception of its surroundings. LiDAR provides a high-precision 3D map of the environment around the vehicle, while radar provides reliable object detection in adverse weather

Die im Kostenmodell gewählten Parameter der Szenarien für Kun- den und Verkehrseigenschaften erscheinen uns zweckmäßig. 62 Vgl. Evans / Filsfils (2007): S. 251f. 63 Vgl. Evans / Filsfils (2007): S. 251f; Siehe auch Hackbarth / Kulenkampff (2006): 68f. 42 Diskussionsbeitrag Nr. 315 Abbildung 3-8: Verkehrszusammensetzung beeinflusst Vorteilhaftigkeit der Priorisierung Echtzeit Best-Effort Überdimensionierung vorteilhafter Priorisierung vorteilhafter Vor dem Hintergrund der Migration zu Next Generation Access mit deutlich höheren Bandbreiten sind die im Modell angelegten Aggregationsbandbreiten eher gering. Inso- fern wird modelltechnisch eher eine aktuelle ADSL-Welt mit DSLAM am Hauptverteiler abgebildet. Legt man die Ergebnisse der Modellierung zugrunde, so scheinen mit den höheren Aggregationsbandbreiten auch weniger Zusatzkosten der QoS-Bereitstellung verbunden zu sein. Allerdings muss berücksichtigt werden, dass die durchschnittliche Bandbreite pro Nutzer durch die neuen Dienste ebenfalls steigt64. Insofern wäre es bei der Weiterentwicklung des Modells interessant, eine NGN-Welt abzubilden, in der hohe Anschlussbandbreiten vorliegen, aus der implizit eine höhere Nutzungsrate z.B. der Echtzeit und Streaming-Klassen bei den Privatkunden aufgrund von erhöhter Triple- Play Penetration abgeleitet werden. Aus aktueller regulatorischer Perspektive sind diese Ergebnisse wertvoll, weil sie deut- lich machen, dass es in den Kernnetzen, wo durchweg nur sehr hohe Bandbreiten ge- nutzt werden, keine signifikanten Kostenunterschied

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways
content.abstract_or_snippet: atability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways. 1. Introduction Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) have seen vast applications in the transportation domain. The integration of Global Positioning System (GPS) and Beidou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) in railway is efficient for modernizing the railway network, GNSS is increasingly becoming a fundamental pillar not only for non-safety applications, but also for im- proved maintenance, automated train operation, and safety- related appli- cations, including the Next Generation of Train Control (NGTC) and re- novation of the train on-board equipment, all these will increase the railway system capacity, efficiency and safety of all railways [1]. To apply GNSS in railway train operation, the GNSS performance testing and verification is the essential procedure for the system to be adopted and further listed as mandatory in the system design and development. The certification of the GNSS-based train localization systems is also necessary to provide concrete validation or testing application services to carry out simulation tests with evaluation of the platform and environmental condi- tion simulations [2]. Beidou has a set of technical and regulatory standards, including the BDS Performance Standard, BDS Signal Interface Control Document (ICD), and technical requirements for user equipment. The BDS Signal In Space (SIS) and service performance characteristics include ac- curacy, availability, compatibility, and interoperability [3]. GPS has also published standard positioning service performance standards to guide the user for civil uses worldwide, the performance metrics are GPS SIS accuracy, continuity, and availability [4]. So both BDS and GPS are focusing on ac- curacy as the foundation for the GNSS performance [5]. The performance needs to be assessed and as the performances are all credited to the equipment, the equipment itself needs to be certified. The certification procedure, it is governed by various international and regional standards, such as the accuracy standards by BDS and GPS, the test and simulation standard procedures, the simulation must meet possible (as sensible to the GNSS) to make the test results plausible. content.title: On-Demand-Ridepooling als Beitrag zur Mobilitätswende und Daseinsvorsorge – Status quo und Systemtypologie.
content.abstract_or_snippet: On-Demand-Ridepooling ermöglicht prinzipiell eine gebündelte Beförderung auch in Gebieten mit relativ geringer Nachfrage. Doch obgleich diese Angebotsform eine deutlich effizientere Nutzung materieller Ressourcen (Autos) ermöglicht als der Pri- vatbesitz, kann On-Demand-Ridepooling heute nur in wenigen Szenarien ohne Son- derförderung eingesetzt werden34. Dies liegt größtenteils am erforderlichen Fahrper- sonal, denn dieses macht derzeit etwa 70 % der Systemkosten aus (siehe Kapitel 3.6). Zudem ist angesichts des Fachkräftemangels auch die Gewinnung von Fahrpersonal sehr herausfordernd und hohe Krankenstände sorgen für Probleme mit der Verfüg- barkeit der Systeme35. Doch die Lösung zeichnet sich klar ab: Früher oder später werden autonome Fahr- zeuge für eine drastische Reduktion der Kosten sorgen und die Suche nach Fahrper- sonal überflüssig machen. Wann dies der Fall sein kann, ist umstritten und wurde schon mehrfach verfrüht vorhergesagt (Mitteregger & Banerjee, 2021). Zwar waren die ersten autonomen Shuttles (begleitet durch Fahrpersonal) schon 2017 in Bad Birnbach unterwegs (zu Erkenntnissen aus diesem Versuch siehe Riener et al., 2020) und der RMV gab 2022 bekannt, dass er seine Fahrzeugflotte in Darmstadt und dem Kreis Offenbach ab 2023 mit von ioki bereitgestellten Fahrzeugen der Automatisie- rungsstufe 4 (eigenständig fahrende Fahrzeuge) „maßgeblich verstärken“ wolle36. Einige Expert*innen sprechen dennoch von ca. 20 Jahren bis zum Regelbetrieb37. Derzeit wird eine Vielzahl an Projekten in diesem Bereich gefördert38, und der VDV führt eine Liste zu On-Demand-Projekten, die an autonomen Shuttles forschen39. Auch die gesetzlichen Rahmenbedingungen wurden angepasst, sodass autonome Fahrzeuge auf Autobah

Abbildung 16: Bedienzeitencluster (alle ermittelten Systeme) Am deutlichsten heben sich die Systeme des Clusters Nachtschwärmer von den anderen Systemen ab. Sie sind wochentags meist bis 1 oder 2 Uhr nachts aktiv, am Wochen- ende etwas länger. Das Cluster Basismobilität umfasst Systeme, die nur einen Teil des Tages bedie- nen. Sie starten ihren Dienst ab 8 oder 9 Uhr, was einen Weg zur Schule oder Arbeit für viele Menschen ausschließt. Zudem haben sie teilweise Mittagspausen oder en- den schon am frühen Nachmittag. Die Differenzierung zwischen den drei übrigen Clustern ist weniger klar. Alle starten wochentags schon am frühen Morgen, meist zwischen 5 und 6 Uhr. Der Typ Ar- beitsshuttle fährt meist bis etwa 19 -21 Uhr. content.title: On-Demand-Ridepooling als Beitrag zur Mobilitätswende und Daseinsvorsorge – Status quo und Systemtypologie.
content.abstract_or_snippet: grund der zeitlichen Konsistenz und einer großen Stichprobe sehr ernst genommen werden. Solche zusätzlichen Wege können verschieden interpretiert werden: aus ökologischer Perspektive sind sie problematisch, da sie die Umweltbelastungen erhöhen, aus sozi- aler Perspektive können sie jedoch ein Indiz für eine gesteigerte soziale Teilhabe sein. Die ökologischen Schäden fallen bei einer Nutzung von E-Fahrzeugen geringer aus, da beim sprinti jedoch etwa drei Viertel der Fahrzeuge mit Verbrennungs-moto- ren angetrieben werden17 und die Fahrzeuge aufgrund ihrer Größe sowohl in der Pro- duktion als auch im Betrieb relativ hohe Ressourcenverbräuche verursachen, sind hier bei einer im ländlichen Raum als gering erwartbaren Poolingquote durchaus Treibhausgas- und Ressourcenverbrauchssteigerungen möglich. Bündelungsquote bzw. Systemeffizienz Ridepooling setzt auf die gebündelte Beförderung mehrerer Personen. Daher wird die sogenannte Bündelungsquote häufig als zentraler Indikator für den Erfolg eines Systems gesehen. Für die Berechnung wird jedoch eine Vielzahl verschiedener Me- thoden eingesetzt: je nachdem, ob der Anteil der Fahrten, auf denen mehrere Fahr- gäste (oder Buchungen) im Fahrzeug sind, ob der Anteil der Kilometer, den mehrere Fahrgäste gemeinsam zurücklegen oder ob die durchschnittliche Anzahl verschiede- ner Fahrgäste (oder Buchungen) im Fahrzeug genutzt wird, ergeben sich unter- schiedliche Werte. Die Angaben der Systembetreiber sind daher oft nicht vergleich- bar. Sowohl aus ökologischer wie aus Kundensicht gibt es jedoch einen Berechnungs- weg, der die Systemperformance am besten beschreibt. Die sogenannte „Systemeffi- zienz“ berechnet sich aus der Summe der kürzesten möglichen Wege aller Fahrgäste zu ihren Zielen (direkte Fahrt) dividiert durch die Zahl der insgesamt gefahrenen Ki- lometer, inklusive Betriebs

Abbildung 16 stellt die Bedienzeiten der 60 durch die Recherche erfassten Systeme zwischen Montag und Sonntag dar. Jede Zeile steht für die Bedienzeiten eines Sys- tems. Voll bediente Stunden sind komplett eingefärbt, Stunden die jeweils bis zur oder ab einer Stundenmitte bedient werden sind schraffiert. Stunden, die nur an ein- zelnen Wochentagen bedient werden sind grau dargestellt. Dabei handelt es sich in der Regel um Montage (Bedienung in die ersten Nachtstunden) oder Freitage (Bedie- nung der späten Abendstunden). Kleinere Abweichungen, wie eine Bedienung mit- tags unter der Woche an nur einem Tag oder längere Fahrzeiten in der Nacht von Sonntag auf Montag, konnten in dieser Darstellung nicht berücksichtigt werden, sind aber für das Gesamtbild auch nur von untergeordneter Relevanz. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. Related works Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap- plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSS- based positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. To address interference-related challenges, an inter- ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech- nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Early in the 1990s, the United States introduces the concept of the Positive Train control System (PTS) [1], which relies on Global Posi- tioning System (GPS), accelerometers, and fiber-optic gyroscopes for the train positioning function. By December 2020, the United States achieved 100 % implementation of Positive Train Control (PTC) across all required railroads, marking a historic milestone in rail safety and interoperability [2]. CLUG 2.0 project [3] launched in 2023 in Europe aims to enhance the European Rail Traffic Management System (ERTMS) by integrating an on-board GNSS+EGNOS-based multi-sensor fusion architecture. In China, the China State Railway Group Co., Ltd. introduced a new train control system, the Chinese Train Control System-New (CTCS-N) [4], which proposed a “train-borne centric” train operation control method. This system incorporates on-board GNSS positioning, speed sensors, and electronic railway maps to achieve train localization. GNSS has been acknowledged as a pivotal enabler in im- proving the cost efficiency and automation level of the train control system. In complex railway operation scenarios, such as urban canyons, tunnels, and stations with cover, the limited GNSS signal visibility, and weak observation geometry would greatly decrease the positioning performance. Thus, the integration solutions are typically proposed for the train safety positioning. A typical integration solution for GNSS- based train positioning is based on the data fusion with both GNSS and Inertial Navigation System (INS) sensors. The GNSS/INS integrated solution is capable of providing full navigation solutions of position, velocity, and attitude in high positioning accuracy, high updating rate even under the GNSS limited scenario when the visible satellites are less than four. Additionally, there is an increasing concern that GNSS is also vulnerable to radio frequency jamming, due to the weak signal power of the GNSS signal. More and more GNSS disturbance events are reported because of jamming, in worldwide, including maritime applications [5,6], aviation applications [7], and also railway applications [8,9]. GNSS in-band interferences, for example, intentional jamming shows a source of potential threats to the safe, secure, and resilient operation of a dense railway system utilizing GNSS as the main positioning method

The integration of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) into railway control systems aims to reduce dependence on track-side in- frastructure. While GNSS has significantly improved train localization, challenges such as the susceptibility to jamming remain. To address this, this paper introduces an Inertial Navigation System (INS)-aided train posi- tioning system based on deep integration, exploring its performance through semi-physical experiments and simulations. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed solution is able to reduce the positioning error by 63.47 %, and the velocity error by 58.47 % under jamming conditions. The study highlights the potential of deep integration for improving the resilience of GNSS-based train control systems, especially in the face of Radio Frequency (RF) jamming threats. 1. Introduction Railway safety and efficiency depend increasingly on knowing every train’s position. Traditional train positioning technologies usually in- volve track circuit positioning, axle counter positioning, and intersec- tion inductive loop positioning. However, these methods are often limited by insufficient positioning accuracy and high maintenance costs. The application of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) in railway train control systems aims to reduce as much as possible the use of track-side infrastructures for train location awareness, including track circuits and Balises. GNSS-based train positioning has been ex- tensively researched worldwide. Specific projects and initiatives have been undertaken to integrate GNSS into the railway systems. Conclusion The application of GNSS technology is one of the representative characteristics of intelligent railway systems in the future. However, there are specific requirements to the GNSS-based train positioning and state perception in the railway signaling applications due to the critical re- quirement to the safety. Thus, the utilization of GNSS has to be enhanced by the specific technical solutions. The sensor data fusion technique is of great significance to enhance the service availability under degraded or even a critical GNSS observing condition. To ensure the compatibility to the existing system specifications, the VB technology enables the in- tegration of the GNSS capabilities into the train control system and meanwhile the train integrity monitoring logic allows the opportunity to realize moving block-based train operation control through the dynamic identification of the track occupancy and safe envelope of the train. By investigating the relationship between the GNSS performance system and the RAMS specification, the performance evaluation of the GNSS-based train positioning can be effectively realized. It can be found that the in- volvement of these key technologies consolidates the autonomy and cap- abilities of the train-borne system, which releases the requirement to the track-side facilities like track circuit and Balises. Jiang et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 It is of great importance to develop specific countermeasures to protect train positioning from jamming-caused GNSS disturbance. Research institutions and GNSS receiver manufacturers have been developing GNSS receivers equipped with anti-jamming capabilities. A survey of state-of-art approaches is introduced, based on the functional stages of the receiver in which they operate. Typically, they could be implemented at the front-end, pre-correction, or post-correlation stages, with others functioning at the navigation processing stage. Spatial fil- tering [9–11], functioning at the front-end stage, uses antenna arrays to point the receiver antenna beam toward the GNSS satellites and away from jammers. In the pre-correction stage, time/frequency filtering [12–14] enables the jamming mitigation before the jamming signal enters the receiver. Vector tracking is a typical approach that works on the post-correction stage. Instead of tracking in-view satellites sepa- rately in the conventional baseband signal processing, vector tracking [15,16] is achieved through feedback of the receiver states from the navigation module to the signal tracking channels as prior information and constraints. Additionally, the inertial-aiding-based strategy at the navigation processing stage provides a different way. INS is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals,

all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: achieve high positioning accuracy, GPS signal interruptions in urban environments and tunnels remain a challenge for these sensors. The IMU can provide information about the vehicle’s attitude, velocity, and direction. The IMU helps determine vehicle motion between GNSS updates and ensures continuous tracking of the vehicle’s path, especially in areas where GNSS signals may be temporarily lost, such as tunnels and dense urban areas. The IMU provides real-time feedback on the ve- hicle’s motion, which is critical for continued tracking, especially in dynamic environments. As the positioning error accumulates during the vehicle’s driving, the IMU signal error accumulation will drift over time, so the IMU needs to continuously correct the estimated position. The data fusion of GNSS and IMU can achieve vehicle state estimation and ensure a continuous positioning process. GNSS and IMU data are often fused with other sensors such as LiDAR and radar to improve positioning accuracy and build a complete picture of the vehicle’s surroundings. By fusing these data sources, autonomous vehicles are able to navigate in a variety of environments, even without direct GNSS signals. There are currently several models for combining GNSS and IMU data. (1) GNSS/IMU integration: combining GNSS data with IMU information can create a dead reckoning system that continuously estimates the vehicle’s position when GNSS signals are unavailable. In this model, the IMU uses accelerometers and gyroscopes to track vehicle motion and orientation to fill in the gaps in GNSS data. (2) Tightly coupled system: in this, GNSS and IMU data are fused and processed in real time. This can improve the accuracy of vehicle positioning, es- pecially in challenging environments where GNSS signals are weak or unstable. (3) Loosely coupled system: in this system, GNSS and IMU operate independently and merge the data in the post-processing stage. Compared with the tightly coupled system, this method is less computationally intensive, but less accurate. World Electr. Veh. J. 2025, 16, 20 8 of 34 To mitigate the limitations of GNSS and IMU, these sensors are often fused with other perception technologies, such as LiDAR, radar, and cameras. GNSS/IMU systems are often integrated with LiDAR and radar sensors to enhance the vehicle’s perception of its surroundings. LiDAR provides a high-precision 3D map of the environment around the vehicle, while radar provides reliable object detection in adverse weather

The main problem and challenge for the test can be illustrated as follows. 1) Train movement simulation: the train movement needs to be si- mulated as identical to the train operation, since when the train operates the GNSS receiver mounted on the train should receive the GNSS signal, and the train moving along the track needs to have the right occupation which needs to be demonstrated on the railway simulation platform. Thus, the train maneuver dynamics should be modeled in accordance with the correct train type. An accurate train dynamics model is fundamental to train movement algorithms. 3.1. GNSS testing standard and regulations As illustrated in the introduction part, the public service performance evaluation of GNSS covers two key aspects: SIS performance and system service performance. As the BeiDou Navigation Satellite System Open Service Performance Standard (BDS-OS-PS-3.0) defines, the BDS service performance should cover 5 core indicators: positioning accuracy, velocity measurement accuracy, timing accuracy, PDOP availability, and positioning service availability, as shown in Fig. 4. In terms of positioning accuracy, the specification clarifies the statistical standard of geographical location mea- surement error and specifies the allowable error range under different geographical areas and environmental conditions. The evaluation of speed measurement accuracy is concerned with the accuracy of the user equip- ment to measure the motion speed, which is used in dynamic scenarios, such as transportation and railway applications. Timing accuracy is aimed at high-precision time synchronization services, which are critical cap- abilities in fields such as communications, energy, and scientific experi- ments. The availability of PDOP is an important index to evaluate the effect of geometric configuration on positioning accuracy and to measure the degree of support of satellite layout to the stability of positioning solution. Location service availability measures the ability to provide continuous and reliable service at a given time and area from the perspective of the overall functionality of the system. In addition, the standard also specifies the measurement methods of this performance, including the evaluation environment, data collection method, calculation method, and evaluation cycle specific requirements. Introduction Global Navigation Satellite Systems (GNSSs) have seen vast applications in the transportation domain. The integration of Global Positioning System (GPS) and Beidou Navigation Satellite System (BDS) in railway is efficient for modernizing the railway network, GNSS is increasingly becoming a fundamental pillar not only for non-safety applications, but also for im- proved maintenance, automated train operation, and safety- related appli- cations, including the Next Generation of Train Control (NGTC) and re- novation of the train on-board equipment, all these will increase the railway system capacity, efficiency and safety of all railways [1]. To apply GNSS in railway train operation, the GNSS performance testing and verification is the essential procedure for the system to be adopted and further listed as mandatory in the system design and development. The certification of the GNSS-based train localization systems is also necessary to provide concrete validation or testing application services to carry out simulation tests with evaluation of the platform and environmental condi- tion simulations [2]. Beidou has a set of technical and regulatory standards, including the BDS Performance Standard, BDS Signal Interface Control Document (ICD), and technical requirements for user equipment. The BDS Signal In Space (SIS) and service performance characteristics include ac- curacy, availability, compatibility, and interoperability [3]. GPS has also published standard positioning service performance standards to guide the user for civil uses worldwide, the performance metrics are GPS SIS accuracy, continuity, and availability [4]. So both BDS and GPS are focusing on ac- curacy as the foundation for the GNSS performance [5]. The performance needs to be assessed and as the performances are all credited to the equipment, the equipment itself needs to be certified. Die meisten Preismodelle versuchen den Preis als Instrument zur Vermeidung von Ü- berlast einzusetzen, indem sie auf die Preissensibilität der Nutzer abstellen.78 In eini- gen Fällen (Paris Metro Pricing, Priority Pricing, Smart-Market Pricing) geschieht dies, indem die Nutzer aufgefordert werden, ihren Verkehr zu bewerten, sodass eine relative Rangreihung nach Prioritäten erfolgen kann. Bei diesen Verfahren kann der Nutzer ge- nerell unbegrenzt viel Verkehr generieren/nachfragen, aber es gibt keine Garantie für die Einhaltung der QoS. In anderen Fällen (Edge Pricing oder Expected Capacity Pri- cing) sind die Verfahren direkt mit der Zugangskontrolle (Admission Control) verknüpft. Bei diesen Preismodellen kann dem Nutzer der Zugang verweigert werden, um das Netz vor Überlast zu schützen. Einige Modelle (z.B. Smart-Market) integrieren eine Komponente für lastabhängige Preise („congestion pricing“), die im Überlastfall dyna- misch zusätzliche / andere Preise setzt. Der Smart-Market Ansatz lässt sich jedoch kaum auf große Netze anwenden.79 In den meisten Fällen kann keine individuelle QoS gewährt werden, weil die Preismo- delle reine Best-Effort Netze zugrunde legen. Die Mehrheit der Preismodelle arbeitet mit kurzen Zeitspannen (im Minutenrahmen), sodass sie flexibel genug auf kurzfristige Ü- berlastsituationen reagieren zu können. Zwei oder mehr Endgeräte verbinden sich zu einem vermaschten Telekommunikationsnetz. Diese Netze bauen sich selbständig auf und konfigurieren sich selbständig. Bei Floating Car Data (FCD) geht es um das Messen und Auswerten der Fahrzeugbewegungen anhand von Daten, die direkt aus dem Fahrzeug kommen. Grundlage ist Global Positioning System (GPS) für die Positionsbestimmung im Fahrzeug und Global System for Mobile Communication (GSM)-Kommunikation für die Übertragung von Informationen zwischen Fahrzeug und Zentrale. Damit entsteht im Fahrzeug eine mitschwimmende Messstation für Verkehrsdaten. In der Zentrale werden die eingehenden anonymisierten Meldungen temporär zwischengespeichert und deren Integrität geprüft. Mit Hilfe von digitalen Karten kann damit der Fahrweg und das Fahrprofil rekonstruiert werden. Die aufbereiteten Daten fließen in ein Rechnersystem zur Zustandsmodellierung der Verkehrslage. Daraus lassen sich aktuelle Verkehrsmeldungen, Verkehrsprognosen und Umfahrungsempfehlungen ableiten. all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: me der kürzesten möglichen Wege aller Fahrgäste zu ihren Zielen (direkte Fahrt) dividiert durch die Zahl der insgesamt gefahrenen Ki- lometer, inklusive Betriebs- und Leerfahrten (siehe Liebchen et al., 2021). Andere Berechnungsmethoden ergeben meist Anreize für Umwege, die die Poolingquote er- höhen, dabei aber die gefahrenen Umwege und teilweise auch Gesamtkilometer er- höhen. Insbesondere in Zeit-Räumen schwacher Nachfrage ist eine Bündelung häufig mit großen Umwegen und daher, im Vergleich zum privaten PKW, unattraktiven Reise- zeiten verbunden (Agora Verkehrswende, 2023, S. 40). Aufgrund der oftmals unkla- ren Definition der angegebenen Bündelungsquoten wird hier auf eine Listung ver- schiedener Werte verzichtet und auf die Analyse der vorliegenden Daten in Kapitel 4.2.2 verwiesen. Leerfahrten An- und Abwege zu Nutzer*innen, kurzfristige Buchungsstornierungen oder ähnli- ches führen zu Wegen, die bei Fahrten mit privaten PKW nicht anfallen. Sie erhöhen somit das Verkehrsaufkommen, steigern die Kosten und verschlechtern die Ökobi- lanz der Dienstleistungen. Inso- fern wird modelltechnisch eher eine aktuelle ADSL-Welt mit DSLAM am Hauptverteiler abgebildet. Legt man die Ergebnisse der Modellierung zugrunde, so scheinen mit den höheren Aggregationsbandbreiten auch weniger Zusatzkosten der QoS-Bereitstellung verbunden zu sein. Allerdings muss berücksichtigt werden, dass die durchschnittliche Bandbreite pro Nutzer durch die neuen Dienste ebenfalls steigt64. Insofern wäre es bei der Weiterentwicklung des Modells interessant, eine NGN-Welt abzubilden, in der hohe Anschlussbandbreiten vorliegen, aus der implizit eine höhere Nutzungsrate z.B. der Echtzeit und Streaming-Klassen bei den Privatkunden aufgrund von erhöhter Triple- Play Penetration abgeleitet werden. all_chunks.title: On-Demand-Ridepooling als Beitrag zur Mobilitätswende und Daseinsvorsorge – Status quo und Systemtypologie.
all_chunks.abstract_or_snippet: On-Demand-Ridepooling ermöglicht prinzipiell eine gebündelte Beförderung auch in Gebieten mit relativ geringer Nachfrage. Doch obgleich diese Angebotsform eine deutlich effizientere Nutzung materieller Ressourcen (Autos) ermöglicht als der Pri- vatbesitz, kann On-Demand-Ridepooling heute nur in wenigen Szenarien ohne Son- derförderung eingesetzt werden34. Dies liegt größtenteils am erforderlichen Fahrper- sonal, denn dieses macht derzeit etwa 70 % der Systemkosten aus (siehe Kapitel 3.6). Zudem ist angesichts des Fachkräftemangels auch die Gewinnung von Fahrpersonal sehr herausfordernd und hohe Krankenstände sorgen für Probleme mit der Verfüg- barkeit der Systeme35. Doch die Lösung zeichnet sich klar ab: Früher oder später werden autonome Fahr- zeuge für eine drastische Reduktion der Kosten sorgen und die Suche nach Fahrper- sonal überflüssig machen. Wann dies der Fall sein kann, ist umstritten und wurde schon mehrfach verfrüht vorhergesagt (Mitteregger & Banerjee, 2021). Zwar waren die ersten autonomen Shuttles (begleitet durch Fahrpersonal) schon 2017 in Bad Birnbach unterwegs (zu Erkenntnissen aus diesem Versuch siehe Riener et al., 2020) und der RMV gab 2022 bekannt, dass er seine Fahrzeugflotte in Darmstadt und dem Kreis Offenbach ab 2023 mit von ioki bereitgestellten Fahrzeugen der Automatisie- rungsstufe 4 (eigenständig fahrende Fahrzeuge) „maßgeblich verstärken“ wolle36. Einige Expert*innen sprechen dennoch von ca. 20 Jahren bis zum Regelbetrieb37. Derzeit wird eine Vielzahl an Projekten in diesem Bereich gefördert38, und der VDV führt eine Liste zu On-Demand-Projekten, die an autonomen Shuttles forschen39. Auch die gesetzlichen Rahmenbedingungen wurden angepasst, sodass autonome Fahrzeuge auf Autobah

Die Analyse ergibt, je nach gewünschter Differenzierung, drei bis fünf verschiedene Bedienzeitencluster. Abbildung 16: Bedienzeitencluster (alle ermittelten Systeme) Am deutlichsten heben sich die Systeme des Clusters Nachtschwärmer von den anderen Systemen ab. Sie bedienen ihr Gebiet an Wochentagen ab den Abendstun- den (Start zwischen 18 und 22 Uhr) und am Wochenende beginnt die Buchbarkeit etwas früher. Sie sind wochentags meist bis 1 oder 2 Uhr nachts aktiv, am Wochen- ende etwas länger. Einige Systeme sind nur an Freitagen und Samstagen (bis in den –––– 21 KVB, ohne Jahr: „Isi: Der On-Demand-Service der KVB“. URL: https://www.kvb.koeln/mobilitaet/isi/index.html, abgerufen am 30.09.2023. 50 | Wuppertal Institut 202_Wuppertal Paper On-Demand-Ridepooling als Beitrag zu Mobilitätswende und Daseinsvorsorge Sonntag) aktiv, drei Systeme bedienen am Sonntag fast den gesamten Tag und bieten hier einen Ersatz für die ausgedienten Linienverkehre. Das Cluster Basismobilität umfasst Systeme, die nur einen Teil des Tages bedie- nen. Sie starten ihren Dienst ab 8 oder 9 Uhr, was einen Weg zur Schule oder Arbeit für viele Menschen ausschließt. Zudem haben sie teilweise Mittagspausen oder en- den schon am frühen Nachmittag. Die Differenzierung zwischen den drei übrigen Clustern ist weniger klar. Alle starten wochentags schon am frühen Morgen, meist zwischen 5 und 6 Uhr. Der Typ Ar- beitsshuttle fährt meist bis etwa 19 -21 Uhr. Er ist somit für typische Arbeitstage sowie für früh endende Abendgestaltungen geeignet. Am Wochenende ist er meist zwischen 7 und 20 Uhr

Solche zusätzlichen Wege können verschieden interpretiert werden: aus ökologischer Perspektive sind sie problematisch, da sie die Umweltbelastungen erhöhen, aus sozi- aler Perspektive können sie jedoch ein Indiz für eine gesteigerte soziale Teilhabe sein. Die ökologischen Schäden fallen bei einer Nutzung von E-Fahrzeugen geringer aus, da beim sprinti jedoch etwa drei Viertel der Fahrzeuge mit Verbrennungs-moto- ren angetrieben werden17 und die Fahrzeuge aufgrund ihrer Größe sowohl in der Pro- duktion als auch im Betrieb relativ hohe Ressourcenverbräuche verursachen, sind hier bei einer im ländlichen Raum als gering erwartbaren Poolingquote durchaus Treibhausgas- und Ressourcenverbrauchssteigerungen möglich. Bündelungsquote bzw. Systemeffizienz Ridepooling setzt auf die gebündelte Beförderung mehrerer Personen. Daher wird die sogenannte Bündelungsquote häufig als zentraler Indikator für den Erfolg eines Systems gesehen. Für die Berechnung wird jedoch eine Vielzahl verschiedener Me- thoden eingesetzt: je nachdem, ob der Anteil der Fahrten, auf denen mehrere Fahr- gäste (oder Buchungen) im Fahrzeug sind, ob der Anteil der Kilometer, den mehrere Fahrgäste gemeinsam zurücklegen oder ob die durchschnittliche Anzahl verschiede- ner Fahrgäste (oder Buchungen) im Fahrzeug genutzt wird, ergeben sich unter- schiedliche Werte. Die Angaben der Systembetreiber sind daher oft nicht vergleich- bar. Sowohl aus ökologischer wie aus Kundensicht gibt es jedoch einen Berechnungs- weg, der die Systemperformance am besten beschreibt. Die sogenannte „Systemeffi- zienz“ berechnet sich aus der Summe der kürzesten möglichen Wege aller Fahrgäste zu ihren Zielen (direkte Fahrt) dividiert durch die Zahl der insgesamt gefahrenen Ki- lometer, inklusive Betriebs

Number of in-view satellites is up to 11. The scenario maintained a sustained velocity between 30 and 35 m/s throughout the trajectory. Scenario II: The trajectory starts at GPS week 2034, second of week 289800, lasting for 160 s. The trajectory also replicated a train journey along the Qinghai–Xizang Railway from Nagqu to Amdo section. A number of in-view satellites is up to 5. The velocity continuously ac- celerated from 25 to 27 m/s. In the simulated test, a GNSS signal at GPS L1 frequency is simulated with a reference level of −130 dBm, while the jamming generator operates at a center frequency of 1575.42 MHz with a power level of −90 dBm. The Jamming-to-Signal Ratio (JSR), i.e., the ratio of the jamming signal power to the GNSS signal power, is used to express the relationship between GNSS signal power and jamming power. Beyond complete signal obstruction scenarios, intermediate jamming power levels can induce significant performance degradation in GNSS re- ceivers without fully blocking satellite signal acquisition. These inter- mediate power levels would be hazardous and difficult to detect and mitigate, making it crucial to accurately assess the impact of jamming on GNSS signal quality. Previous research [18] has demonstrated sub- stantial signal quality degradation under jamming conditions with JSR values ranging from 38 to 54 dB. Accordingly, this study focuses on a 40 dB JSR scenario, corresponding to the specified −130 dBm GPS signal and −90 dBm jamming power configuration. A 5 Hz ramp waveform is employed, sweeping ± 1 kHz around the carrier. The jamming signal for investigating the collected IF signal, and the software-defined radio GNSS receiver are used to analyze the data. This software receiver is configured with a second-order phase-locked loop and a second-order code delay lock loop. The carrier loop band- width is set to 3 Hz, while the code loop bandwidth is maintained at 3 Hz. In the proposed solution, INS mechanization updates every 1 ms, while the tracking loop

[23–25], based on the time-frequency behavior, jamming signals can be typically divided into several classes, including Continuous Wave (CW), AM, FM, Frequency Sweep (FS; also referred to is the sampling interval, and as chirp jamming), Narrow-Band Gaussian white Noise (NBGN), and Wideband Gaussian white Noise (WBGN). It is found in Ref. [25] that the majority of the jammers use chirp signals, which vary the signal frequency linearly over time . The chirp signal exhibits a constant rate of frequency change over time, within a certain frequency range and sweep period. This sweep procedure is characterized by the initial and frequency , and the sweep period T. Thus, the instantaneous frequency is , the minimum and maximum frequency at given by The time-domain representation of the chirp signal is modeled as (2) (3) The jamming signals will not only disrupt the proper functioning of the receiver’s RF front-end components, such as low-noise amplifiers, automatic gain control, and analog-to-digital converters, but also ad- versely affect the operation of the carrier and code tracking loops. This jamming can prevent the receiver from acquiring and maintaining a stable lock on the satellite signals, ultimately leading to degraded po- sitioning performance. 3.2. INS-aided jamming protection solution Fig. 2 illustrates the INS-aided GNSS tracking loop. The carrier signal measurement enters the tracking loop through the phase dis- criminator to generate a phase error estimate. This phase error drives the carrier loop filter which outputs an estimate of the signal Doppler to drive the carrier controller oscillator (NCO). The NCO duplicates the phase of the incoming signal, and this phase estimate is fed back to the phase discriminator. The tracking loop is usually said to be “locked” if the phase error is less than five degrees. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: - fectively simulating realistic amplitude fluctuations observed in Radio Frequency (RF) interference sources. A similar signal interference set- ting is illustrated in Ref. [23]. Dynamic amplitude variations can sig- nificantly degrade GNSS signal reception, particularly in high-noise or contested environments. The transition FM Interference simulates frequency-modulated signal interference by generating two sinusoidal components with symmetrical frequency deviations around a carrier frequency centered at 1575.42 MHz, corresponding to the GNSS L1 band. These deviations are sampled from a Gaussian distribution with a standard deviation of 75 kHz, emulating the spectral characteristics of typical FM broadcast signals [24]. This approach captures the dynamic spectral spreading commonly observed in real-world RF interference. The transition Pulse Interference generates periodic pulse signals to si- mulate sudden and intense bursts of RF energy. It samples two random parameters: the pulse width Tp which represents the duration of each pulse, and the pulse interval Ti which defines the spacing between successive pulses. A uniformly distributed amplitude between 1 and 5 simulates the varying severity of the interference. At each time step, the model checks whether the current time modulo the total pulse period Tp + Ti falls within the pulse’s active duration. If so, an interference signal of the specified amplitude is emitted; otherwise, it remains zero. This periodic pulsed structure reflects typical burst-like interference patterns observed in GNSS environments [25], characterized by sharp, short-lived increases in signal strength followed by rapid attenuation. The input data enter the module through the input places Open Area and GNSS Signal, representing the GNSS satellites and the initial en- vironmental conditions. The place Bool Control and transition Mutex manage the logic for introducing signal interference, with the code in Mutex ensuring that the generation processes for different interference types do not conflict. Each interference signal is integrated into the GNSS data by firing the transition Interference. The GNSS data affected by signal interferences proceed to the observation output stage con- trolled by the place GNSS Number. The final results are outputted from the output place GNSS Observation. 3.2.2. Submodule Mountain Fig. 6 illustrates the shielding effect of mountainous terrain on GNSS signals. In addition to incorporating AM, FM, and pulse signal inter- ferences, the module introduces environmental factors of mountain height, terrain data, and obstruction distance. The setup of the GNSS jamming test platform is shown in Fig. 4, consisting of several key components, each connected through specific signal transmission paths, designed to enable jamming data collection for analytical processing. The Spirent GSS8000 GNSS simulator and the sight N5172B jamming signal generator are con- trolled from the simulator’s SimGEN host controller, which generates the required GPS L1 RF signals and the jamming signals, respectively. The jamming signals are defined, along with all the other scenario parameters, within the SimGEN. The scenario trajectory collected in the field is input into the SimGEN software, driving the simulator to generate the corre- sponding dynamic GNSS RF signals. A signal combiner is utilized to in- tegrate the clean GPS RF signal and the specific jamming signal. Since the down-converted Intermediate Frequency (IF) signal is what we need, the HG-softGPS06 GNSS IF signal collector is connected to the simulated signal to capture the necessary data, which is then collected and post-processed by the PC host. Field test results from a train location system were collected and used as the scenario script to drive the controller. A tactical-grade SPAN system (a high-precision GNSS/INS integrated system with a tactical grade, low noise IMU-FSAS) installed on the train was utilized to collect the tra- jectory and the IMU data. Two practical local trajectories that last for 160 s are adopted as the operation script to generate different GNSS jamming tested scenarios and drive the GNSS simulator. The key parameters of the trajectory are listed as follows. Scenario I: The trajectory starts at GPS week 2034, second of week 288888, lasting for 160 s. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - INS-aided GNSS jamming protection in support of resilient train positioning
content.abstract_or_snippet: is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals, which makes it possible to benefit from the mitigation of GNSS jamming signals. In the railway domain, an extensive analysis of interference events based on a one-year dataset collected across the Swiss railway network was con- ducted in Ref. [8], which revealed widespread occurrences of GNSS interference, particularly clustered around larger cities and densely populated residential areas. A test and evaluation of GNSS-based railway positioning under various jamming conditions was presented in Ref. [17], demonstrating the susceptibility of train localization perfor- mance to interference. A resilient localization framework operating at the measurement level, incorporating jamming discrimination and multi-sensor fusion, was proposed in Ref. [18]. Nevertheless, research on GNSS anti-jamming solutions for railway environments remains re- latively limited, which motivates the focus of the present work. GNSS/INS integration can be categorized into three primary archi- tectures: loosely coupled, tightly coupled, and deeply coupled sys- tems [19,20]. In a loosely coupled integration, the GNSS receiver and INS operate independently, with integration occurring at the navigation solution level by combining the position and velocity outputs from both systems. This approach requires a minimum of four visible satellites for a positioning solution, which can be a limitation in environments with jamming signals. In contrast, tightly-coupled integration integrates raw GNSS measurements, such as pseudoranges and Doppler shifts, directly with INS solution within a filter. This method allows the system to utilize all available satellite signals, even when fewer than four sa- tellites are visible, enhancing performance in challenging environ- ments. Deeply coupled, or ultra-tight, integration goes a step further by incorporating inertial measurements directly into the GNSS signal tracking loops. This integration aids in maintaining signal lock under high dynamics and interference conditions, as the INS can provide predictive information to the GNSS receiver’s tracking algorithms. The advantages of deep integration are particularly evident in environments with significant interference or high dynamics. In Ref. [21], the anti- jamming performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-j

This paper introduces a zero on-site testing scheme for GNSS perfor- mance evaluation for railway train control system adoption. The paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the problems and challenges in the methods for zero on-site testing, Section 3 introduces the key technol- ogies in the testing procedure. After that, Section 4 introduces the data collected using the zero on-site testing platform, and Section 5 delivers the conclusion of the testing performance and characteristics. 2) GNSS signal propagation: with the train movement, the GNSS signal propagation in the real world can be affected by the environment along the railway track. As the optimized signal to be propagated is the Line-Of-Sight (LOS) signal, due to the obstacles along the track, the GNSS signal might be reflected, refracted, and blocked. Thus, environmental modelling is the basis for simulation. There are still many challenges in addressing scientific problems. The main problems to be discussed in this paper is the environmental modelling forming the ray-tracing procedure, and the train movement that integrates the environment and train location to generate the ap- propriate GNSS signal for the zero on-site testing. 2. content.abstract_or_snippet: . S. Chen, D. Wu, J. Liu et al. High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184 Fig. 5. Submodule Open Area. to filter out signals from satellites that are obstructed by the terrain. The places Mountain Height and Distance store mountain elevations and spatial relationships between the terrain and GNSS signal paths. These serve as the foundational data for modeling and analyzing how terrain features obstruct satellite signals. The transition Mountain Data Fusion and its code are responsible for integrating and transmitting these data. The place GNSS Signal stores the initial GNSS satellite data. The transition Mountain Scenario integrates mountain height and obstruc- tion distance information with current visible satellite data to filter out obstructed signals. This process assesses satellite visibility by com- paring each satellite’s elevation angle with the obstruction angle de- rived from the mountain height and the receiver’s distance to the mountain. If a satellite’s elevation angle is lower than the obstruction angle, it is deemed as obstructed and excluded from the positioning calculations. After the firing of the transition Mountain Scenario, the GNSS data are stored in the place Data with Mountain Error. Additionally, visible satellite data are combined with interference sig- nals and output to the place GNSS Observation. By incorporating a mountainous terrain scenario, this module effectively simulates the dynamic impact of terrain obstructions on GNSS signal visibility in complex environments. 3.2.3. Submodule Tunnel Fig. 7 illustrates the process of GNSS signal reception and error variation for a train in a tunnel scenario. It focuses on the dynamic impact of the tunnel environment on the satellite signal visibility and positioning accu- racy. In this module, the places InTunnel, Just Out, and Out Tunnel re- present the train’s current state, including three phases: inside the tunnel, just outside the tunnel, and completely outside the tunnel. Inside the tunnel, a train is shielded by the structure blocking GNSS signal reception. During this phase, the place GNSS Signal remains empty indicating the complete obstruction of satellite sig- nals. When the train just exits the tunnel, the GNSS signals become visible again. However, due to the complex environmental condi- tions near the tunnel exit, such as multipath effects, signal reflec- tions, and delays, the GNSS observation data contain significant er- rors. This process is modeled by the code of the transition Tunnel Scenario, which integrates the tunnel state from the place Tunnel with visible satellite information.

content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: terrain, with the elevation direction being particularly sensitive to these factors. Tunnel scenarios show the most pronounced degradation with a mean error of 5.6670 m. This dramatic increase results from severe signal blockages in tunnels and the instability during signal reacquisition after exiting the tunnel. Fig. 10 illustrates the evolution of position error in the tunnel en- vironment. During the “Inside the Tunnel” phase, GNSS signals are completely blocked by the surrounding structure, resulting in a loss of positioning capability (represented by the gray shaded region). Upon exiting the tunnel (marked by the vertical dashed line), the system begins to reacquire GNSS signals; however, due to severe multipath effects and signal obstructions, the initial position errors are sig- nificantly large. As the train moves further away from the tunnel exit, the surrounding environment improves, leading to a gradual reduction in error, which eventually stabilizes. This trend highlights the critical Fig. 10. Position errors in tunnel scenario. impact of tunnel environments and their transitions on GNSS-based positioning performance. 5. Conclusion This paper presents a CPNs-based approach to evaluate the perfor- mances of GNSS-based train positioning systems under real-world op- erational conditions. By constructing a hierarchical CPN model, the study successfully models the process of GNSS signal reception and interferences, as well as the impacts of complex environmental factors on the GNSS-based train positioning. The simulation results emphasize that signal interferences (i.e., interfered by AM, FM, and pulse signals) have a significant impact on the GNSS-based positioning accuracy, with FM interference exerting the most pronounced effects, particularly in the vertical direction. Environmental factors like terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding further degrade signal visibility and increase po- sitioning errors. The model also identifies critical challenges during signal reacquisition, particularly in tunnel transitions, which exacerbate error growth. While the proposed approach effectively demonstrates the impact of various signal interferences and environmental conditions on the per- formance of the GNSS-based train positioning, future research could explore the integration of additional signal interference mitigation techniques to improve the accuracy and stability of the GNSS-based positioning for railway applications. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 116–124 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article , Wenzheng Qiaoa, Baigen Caia,b,c, Jian Wanga,b,c, Jiang Liua,b,c, Yuchen Zhaoa, Zero on-site testing on GNSS for train control towards digital railways Debiao Lua,b,c,⁎ Chunjie Qiaod, Dirk Spiegele a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c Beijing Engineering Center for EMC and GNSS Technologies, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Hunan Matrix Electronic Technology Co., Ltd., Changsha 410205, China e DB InfraGO AG, Berlin 10557, Germany ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS testing Railway train localization 3D environment modelling Digital track map Zero on-site testing The integration of Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) technology into railway train control systems is a crucial step toward achieving the vision of a digital railway. Traditional train control systems undergo extensive in-house tests and prolonged field tests for certification and approval before operational deployment, leading to high costs, delays, and operational disruptions. This paper introduces a GNSS-based train control localization framework which eliminates the need for on-site testing by leveraging train movement dynamics and 3D en- vironment modeling to create a zero on-site testing platform. The proposed framework simulates train move- ment and the surrounding 3D environment using collected railway line location data and environmental attri- butes to generate realistic multipath signals and obscuration effects. This approach enables comprehensive laboratory-based case studies for train localization, reducing the huge amount test of needed for physical field trials. The framework is established in house, using the data collected at the Test Base of China Academy of Railway Sciences (Circular Railway). Results from the open area and cutting environment tests demonstrate high localization accuracy repeatability within the simulated environment, validating the feasibility and effectiveness of zero on-site testing for GNSS-based train control systems. This research highlights the potential of GNSS simulation platforms in enhancing cost efficiency, operational safety, and accuracy for future digital railways.

High-speed Railway 1 (2023) 47–55 and the Doppler radar, also show great potentials in realizing the au- tonomous train positioning with a low cost over the Balise-based solu- tions [4]. However, it can be found that the single-sensor-based solution cannot satisfy the requirement of the train control system due to specific limitations and disadvantages in practical scenarios [5]. How to explore the capabilities of multiple perception sensors and break through the application-demand-oriented information fusion processing technology is the key to realize the satisfaction of novel train control systems with the train-centric design. Hence, it is important to overcome the key technologies of autonomous train positioning based on the fusion of GNSS and assistant sensors. By developing a novel train control system with dynamic configuration of the trains’ interval, reduced involvement of track-side equipments, and enhanced maintainability of the on-board train positioning system, the advantages of the train-centric mode will be practically reflected and utilized for specific railway lines. In this paper, key technologies of the novel train control system with GNSS- based autonomous perception are investigated and demonstrated in the practical environment, which illustrate the necessity and advantages of the utilization of GNSS, especially BDS (BeiDou Navigation Satellite System), in future safety-related railway systems in China. 2. Multi-sensor data fusion In existing railway signaling systems, the track occupancy of the train is determined by the track circuit, and the exact along-track lo- cation of the train is calculated by integrating both the train-borne odometer (or Doppler radar) and track-side Balises. The novel train- centric signaling system determines the track occupancy and train lo- cation without relying on those track-side facilities. Thus, raw mea- surements from multiple train-borne sensors will be integrated to achieve the required accuracy level, reduce the uncertainty and en- hance the dependability. 2.1. content.abstract_or_snippet: measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. Some studies have achieved high positioning accuracy in partially obstructed or challenging environ- ments by fusing multi-sensor data [6,14,15]. In contrast to studies that mainly investigate GNSS signal inter- ferences in isolation, this paper integrates environmental factors and signal interferences into the formal model of the positioning system. By utilizing model simulation, the proposed approach enables a quantita- tive evaluation of the system’s performance. This method provides a systematic analysis and verification of the interactions among signal interferences, environmental conditions, and train positioning. Due to the rigorous mathematical foundation, formal methods have gained recognition as effective tools for system modeling and verification. In this work, we adopt CPNs [16] as the formal modeling framework to study the impact of signal interferences and environ- mental factors on GNSS-based train positioning. Petri nets are formal modeling languages that are well-known for systems characterized as concurrent, synchronous, distributed, etc. Some application examples adopting petri nets as the means of description for railway signaling systems can be found in Refs. [17–22]. 3. System modeling with CPNs Fig. 1 illustrates the modeling framework of a GNSS-based train positioning system comprising the following main parts: GNSS Re- ceiver, Environment Scenarios, Interference Signals, GNSS Position Solution, and Evaluation Module. The module GNSS Receiver selects specific environment scenarios and receives GNSS signals. The module Environment Scenarios is composed of three submodules: Open Area Scenario, Mountain Occlusion Scenario, and Tunnel and Exit Tunnel Scenario. These submodules simulate various real-world conditions while generating GNSS signal interferences, i.e., GNSS signals are interfered with by AM, FM, and pulse signals. In the module GNSS Positioning Solution, the EKF algorithm is employed to process interfered GNSS signals for estimating the train’s position. The module Evaluation compares the calculated position with the reference position and outputs the positioning error. Fig. 2 clearly illustrates the hierarchical relationships between modules of the CPN model, providing a structured view of their layered organization. The declarations of the model are categorized and summarized in two tables for clarity. Table 1 provides the colset declarations and their respective descriptions, while Table 2 provides the variable declara- tions and their respective descriptions. 3.1. content.title: High-speed Railway 3 - Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets
content.abstract_or_snippet: High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 175–184 Contents lists available at ScienceDirect High-speed Railway journal homepage: www.keaipublishing.com/en/journals/high-speed-railway Research article Modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning system with colored petri nets Shuting Chena,b, Daohua Wua,b,⁎ a School of Automation and Intelligence, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China b National Research Center of Railway Safety Assessment, Collaborative Innovation Center of Railway Traffic Safety, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China c State Key Laboratory of Advanced Rail Autonomous Operation, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China d Frontiers Science Center for Smart High-speed Railway System, Beijing Jiaotong University, Beijing 100044, China , Jiang Liua,c,d, Siqi Wanga,d ]]]] ]]]]]] A R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T Keywords: GNSS-based train positioning Positioning performance Environment scenario GNSS signal interference Colored petri Nets Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-based continuous and accurate train positioning is one of the key technologies for advanced train operations such as train virtual coupling. However, GNSS-based train posi- tioning faces significant challenges in real-world scenarios due to environmental complexities and signal in- terferences. Considering this issue, this paper presents an approach for modeling and performance analysis of GNSS-based train positioning systems using Colored Petri Nets (CPNs). By systematically modeling the GNSS signal reception and processing process, the performance of the positioning system under various environment scenarios is evaluated. The system model integrates three types of interference signals (i.e., Amplitude Modulation (AM) signals, Frequency Modulation (FM) signals, and pulse signals) while incorporating environ- mental factors such as terrain obstructions and tunnel shielding. Additionally, the Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) algorithm is employed to process GNSS observation data, providing accurate train position estimations. The simulation results demonstrate that signal interferences and complex environmental conditions significantly affect the GNSS-based positioning accuracy. This study offers a comprehensive framework for evaluating the performance of GNSS-based train positioning systems in different scenarios, highlighting critical factors that influence positioning accuracy and stability. 1. Introduction With the increasing demand for operation safety and efficiency in rail transportation, train virtual coupling has been proposed and be- come a hot research topic in recent years. For virtual coupling

High-speed Railway 3 (2025) 185–193 information from both the INS and GNSS pseudorange/pseudorange rate, where a specific estimator (e.g., nonlinear Kalman filter) would be adopted. Based on this traditional tightly-coupled GNSS/INS integra- tion, the estimated navigation states are used to estimate the Doppler shift information, which is fed back to the GNSS tracking loop to aid the tracking process. The system operates in a closed-loop way where the integrated filter continuously updates the system’s state estimation by combining data from both the GNSS and INS, and the feedback of the estimated states helps improve the accuracy of both the INS and GNSS calculations. The INS-aided deep integration system leverages the in- ertial data, effectively narrowing the bandwidth of the tracking loop, which helps reduce the noise within the loop and suppress external jamming signals. The system’s capability to maintain accurate tracking and position estimation in jamming environments is significantly strengthened, leading to a more resilient navigation solution. 3. Jamming analysis and protection 3.1. Jamming analysis The received signal strength of the GNSS receiver is extremely low and falls well below the thermal noise floor. Typically, the received GPS signal levels fall within the range of −127 dBm [22]. The weak char- acteristics of GNSS signals will lead to an increasing risk of performance degradation under jamming conditions. Jamming signals may emanate from telecommunication and electronic systems operating in adjacent bands or in bands relatively far from the GNSS bands, i.e., Fre- quency Modulation (FM)/TV transmitter harmonics, Amplitude Mod- ulation (AM) transmitters, and mobile phone networks. The main contributions of this paper are as follows. 1) A novel CPN-based modeling framework for GNSS-based train po- sitioning systems is developed. 2) Dynamic influences of signal interferences (i.e., by AM signals, FM signals, pulse signals) and environmental obstructions (i.e., by mountains and tunnels) are incorporated into the model. 3) The performance of the GNSS-based train positioning system is quantitatively evaluated under various scenarios with the CPN model of the system. 2. Related works Over the past decades, GNSS-based systems have been extensively ap- plied to train positioning due to their global coverage and high accuracy under favorable conditions [1,7]. Despite these advantages, challenges such as signal interferences and terrain-induced occlusions continue to pose questions on high positioning accuracy and stability. Numerous studies have explored techniques to mitigate the impacts of signal interferences on GNSS- based positioning accuracy [8–12]. For example, Ref. [9] investigates the impact of continuous wave interference on GNSS receiver tracking perfor- mance, examining the extent of its influence by varying data bit durations or integration times. To address interference-related challenges, an inter- ference energy detection method and a comprehensive orientation tech- nology tailored for GNSS are introduced in Ref. [11]. Besides, a number of researches have examined the impact of environmental factors along railway lines on GNSS signal integrity. For instance, Ref. [13] explores methods of scenario-based weighted measurement noise calculation and fault detection combined with state recognition based on integrity evalua- tion in autonomous train positioning systems. content.abstract_or_snippet: performance of the INS velocity aiding tracking loop is eval- uated and compared, using a continuous wave jamming scenario. While the superiority of INS-aided deep integration has been preliminarily validated in jamming scenarios, current research on anti-jamming methods remains constrained by simple jamming models such as con- tinuous wave jamming. The mechanisms under different jamming modulation schemes remain underexplored. This study expands the exploration of jamming types by focusing on sweep-frequency inter- ference. Considering the compatibility of the GNSS/INS deeply-coupled integration with the current GNSS-based train positioning function in the control system conception, this paper proposes an INS-aided train determination system (LDS) based on the deep integration. The system profile and the GNSS jamming signals are briefly introduced firstly. Then, we give a detailed introduction of deeply-coupled integration design. Finally, a hardware simulator is used and semi-physical ex- periments are conducted to assess the proposed system’s performance with a sweep frequency jamming scenario. 2. System design From Fig. 1, it can be found that multiple information resources are involved in the train positioning system, where the inertial data from the accelerometers and gyroscopes is taken into the Position, Velocity, and Timing (PVT) calculation to enhance the resilience to abnormal jamming attacks. By integrating an INS directly into the train onboard subsystem, it becomes unnecessary to install or modify the track-side subsystem, thereby simplifying system design and maintenance, thereby providing a flexible architecture for modern rail train control systems. From a signal processing perspective, jamming signals are collected and analyzed together with the authentic GNSS signals by the antenna and the subsequent front-end stage in the GNSS receiver simulta- neously. The signal contaminated by jamming signals would directly affect the subsequent stages of the receiver’s processing chain, in- cluding acquisition and tracking stages. In the tracking stage, jamming prevents the code and carrier loops from maintaining stable signal tracking, ultimately resulting in pronounced errors or even failure in the PVT solution. In the proposed system, the Doppler shift information derived from INS is fed back to aid the GNSS tracking loop, improving the overall resilience of the navigation solution. Inertial mechanization is used to infer Inertial Solution at discrete epochs, while the GNSS tracking loop operates simultaneously until they meet the integration epoch. Research institutions and GNSS receiver manufacturers have been developing GNSS receivers equipped with anti-jamming capabilities. A survey of state-of-art approaches is introduced, based on the functional stages of the receiver in which they operate. Typically, they could be implemented at the front-end, pre-correction, or post-correlation stages, with others functioning at the navigation processing stage. Spatial fil- tering [9–11], functioning at the front-end stage, uses antenna arrays to point the receiver antenna beam toward the GNSS satellites and away from jammers. In the pre-correction stage, time/frequency filtering [12–14] enables the jamming mitigation before the jamming signal enters the receiver. Vector tracking is a typical approach that works on the post-correction stage. Instead of tracking in-view satellites sepa- rately in the conventional baseband signal processing, vector tracking [15,16] is achieved through feedback of the receiver states from the navigation module to the signal tracking channels as prior information and constraints. Additionally, the inertial-aiding-based strategy at the navigation processing stage provides a different way. INS is a self- contained navigation approach in which measurements provided by accelerometers and gyroscopes are used to track the states relative to a known starting state. A remarkable advantage of an INS is that it is inherently immune to Radio Frequency (RF) signals,
